summary: Jeff Atwood continues a dialogue with James Bach about the value of following instructions, responding to Bach's critique of his earlier 'paint can' post. Bach correctly identified Atwood's hidden assumption that painting skill only meant physical application technique, not the broader knowledge of when to prime or use certain materials. While Bach argues that most instructions are useless or harmful, Atwood counters that in practice, we rarely have access to experts and must rely on written guidance. He illustrates this with a real example where remembering a tip about Flotrol paint conditioner solved an actual problem. The post concludes that while questioning instructions is important, sometimes a bulleted list is genuinely helpful.
key_points: Bach's skill at identifying hidden assumptions is valuable - Atwood didn't realize his definition of 'painting skill' excluded following preparation instructions
Most instructions we encounter are ignored (click-wrap licenses, quickstart guides, shampoo bottles saying 'repeat') and many would be harmful if followed
A paint can is neither a supervisor, mentor, nor judge of quality - static instructions can't replace hands-on expert guidance
In practice, experts aren't available and bulleted lists are often all you have to work with
Atwood assumed readers would treat instructions as suggestions and guidelines, not recipes to follow slavishly
money_quotes: Being under Bach's intensive analytical microscope feels a lot like an interview with Hannibal Lecter. It's flattering, but it's also a little scary.
I have tremendous respect for Bach and the way he aggressively questions convention.
Mr. Bach is incredibly effective at sussing out all the assumptions you're making that you don't realize you're making.
I would have to say that most instructions I come across are unimportant and some are harmful.
Some things that look like instructions are not properly instructions at all, they are really just ritual phrases uttered to dispel the evil spirits of legal liability.
A paint can is neither a supervisor, nor a mentor, nor a judge of quality.
Most of the time, you're on your own, and the bulleted list is all you have.
But sometimes a bulleted list of instructions sure does come in handy.
key_insight: While we should question all instructions critically, in the absence of expert guidance, a well-written bulleted list can be surprisingly valuable for solving real problems.
